preFix.title: What compostable packaging actually means
preFix.subtitle: When it actually composts and when it goes to landfill anyway
preFix.excerpt: Packaging labelled compostable falls into two categories: home-compostable (breaks down in a garden bin within 6-12 weeks) and industrially compostable (requires a commercial facility at 60 degrees C and does not break down in a home bin). Most compostable packaging on UK shelves is the industrial category.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP compostable packaging guidance for consumers (2023). European Bioplastics EN 13432 and EN 14045 certification standards. WRAP/Green Alliance compostable plastics in UK waste streams report (2022).

content.text: The word compostable on packaging does not tell you what to do with it. The distinction that matters is whether it is
. Most compostable packaging on UK supermarket shelves is the industrial type.
The problem with industrial-only compostable packaging

Industrial compostable packaging requires a commercial composting facility at high temperature. The UK has limited capacity for this. Most council food waste goes to anaerobic digestion plants, which handle organic food waste but not packaging films. Compostable packaging placed in food waste bins either disrupts the AD process or passes through undigested and goes to landfill.
What to do with each type

Home-compostable packaging: add to the home compost bin. In a hot heap it will break down within two to three weeks. In a cold heap it takes six to twelve weeks. Small items like uncoated paper tea bags and plant-fibre nets will break down in any heap.

Industrially compostable packaging with no local facility: general waste bin. Placing it in recycling contaminates the plastic stream. It looks like conventional plastic but does not work in standard recycling. Placing it in food waste will likely result in landfill.
Industrially compostable packaging where your council runs a dedicated collection: check their waste guide by material type. This is rare, but a few councils are trialling it.
Seedling logo with HOME text: home compostable.
No certification mark: no regulatory claim. The word compostable without a certification mark is an unsubstantiated claim under the Green Claims Code and should be treated as unverified.
Compostable packaging still has a carbon cost to make. Using less packaging first, and choosing recyclable options, is better than relying on any end-of-life fix.
